ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken noodle soup class (FOODON_00002700); no specific entry for Vietnamese-style pho or Kampong chicken variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers chicken soup broadly; no specific pho or Vietnamese chicken noodle soup code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared food dishes at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (coriander, spring onion, Thai basil)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Pho Kampong Chicken Noodle Soup weighing approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, sliced kampong chicken, and fresh herb garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle soup, Vietnamese style (Pho Ga) — nutrient profile estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference values for chicken broth, rice noodles, and lean chicken
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Pho Kampong Chicken Noodle Soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values estimated by component analysis: lean kampong chicken (~30% by weight), rice noodles (~25%), chicken broth (~40%), herbs and condiments (~5%). Per-100g values derived from weighted average of FAO/INFOODS SEA entries for chicken (cooked, lean), rice noodles (cooked), and chicken broth. Sodium elevated due to fish sauce and broth seasoning.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; applied to pho as analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pho Kampong Chicken Noodle Soup. GI estimated at 40 based on cooked rice noodles (GI ~40–50, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source. The substantial broth and protein content further attenuate glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 x 35) / 100 = 14.0, where carbohydrate per 500g serving is approximately 35g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoned broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single 500g serving provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and broth volume
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in moderation; the estimated glycemic load of 14 per serving is moderate, and the lean kampong chicken protein content helps attenuate postprandial glucose rise, but portion size of rice noodles should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content (~2100 mg per serving) and consider requesting less broth or a lower-sodium preparation, while the lean kampong chicken provides a relatively low saturated fat profile compared to red-meat alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Kampong Chicken Noodle Soup (Phở Gà Kampong) is a Vietnamese-inspired chicken noodle soup adapted for the Singapore market, featuring free-range kampong chicken as its centrepiece. The dish consists of a clear, aromatic broth slow-simmered with kampong chicken bones, ginger, star anise, and fish sauce, served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) and topped with sliced poached kampong chicken, bean sprouts, fresh coriander, spring onion, and Thai basil. A wedge of lime and chilli slices are typically served on the side. Kampong chicken, a traditional free-range breed common in Southeast Asia, yields a leaner, more flavourful meat than commercial broiler chicken, contributing to a lower fat content and a richer, more complex broth. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 62 kcal, 6.2g protein, 1.4g fat, and 7.0g carbohydrate, making it a relatively low-calorie, protein-moderate meal. The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100g; ~2100 mg per 500g bowl) due to the seasoned broth and fish sauce, which is a primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), based on the rice noodle component, and the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 14 (moderate). This dish is suitable for individuals seeking a lighter, protein-containing meal, but sodium management is essential for those with blood pressure or heart conditions. It is dairy-free and generally halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.
common_user_queries: Is kampong chicken pho low GI?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Pho Kampong Chicken Noodle Soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy reference values for kampong chicken, cooked rice noodles, and chicken broth. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved source entry exists for this exact dish. Values should be verified against HPB Singapore Foodo if a direct entry becomes available. 2. Sodium content is a primary clinical concern and may vary significantly by restaurant preparation and broth concentration. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy sources used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lean poultry and rice noodle preparations per USDA SR Legacy. 7. GI set to 40 as an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); glycemic load per 500g serving calculated as 14.0. 8. The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine as it is served in Singapore with local adaptations, though its culinary origin is Vietnamese.
